Fixes #48560 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T01:09:17Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes a macOS default browser detection failure where LaunchServices stores a different bundle ID (e.g., `com.microsoft.edgemac`) than what `osascript`'s `path to application id` lookup expects (e.g., `com.microsoft.Edge`), causing the browser to not be detected even though it is installed and set as default. The fix introduces a `KNOWN_BUNDLE_ID_PATHS` map that covers LaunchServices-style bundle IDs for common Chromium-based browsers and updates `detectDefaultChromiumExecutableMac()` to fall back to these hardcoded paths whenever the `osascript`/`defaults` resolution chain fails. The fallback correctly validates file existence before accepting the path, and the two-stage approach (try dynamic resolution first, then fall back) is the right design. **Key changes:** - Adds `KNOWN_BUNDLE_ID_PATHS` mapping LaunchServices bundle IDs (e.g., `com.microsoft.edgemac*`, `com.brave.Browser`, `com.google.Chrome`, etc.) to their conventional `/Applications/...` executable paths. - Expands the early-exit `isKnownChromium` check to accept any bundle ID present in either `CHROMIUM_BUNDLE_IDS` or `KNOWN_BUNDLE_ID_PATHS`. - Restructures `detectDefaultChromiumExecutableMac` to attempt dynamic resolution first, then fall back to the map. **Minor gap:** The fallback map only covers system-wide `/Applications/...` installs. User-level installs under `~/Applications/...` (already handled by the static `findChromeExecutableMac`) are not covered, so the fallback could still miss browsers installed to the user's home directory.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge — the fallback logic is sound and the change is additive, with no risk of breaking existing detection paths. - The core logic is correct: it preserves the existing `osascript`/`defaults` path as the primary resolution and only falls back to hardcoded paths on failure, always verifying file existence before returning. The only notable limitation is that the hardcoded paths cover `/Applications/...` only and not `~/Applications/...` user installs, but this is a minor gap for the targeted edge case (Edge bundle ID mismatch) and the broader fallback (`findChromeExecutableMac`) still covers home-dir installs. - No files require special attention.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/browser/chrome.executables.ts Line: 177-194 Comment: **`~/Applications` user installs not covered in fallback map** The `KNOWN_BUNDLE_ID_PATHS` fallback only contains system-wide `/Applications/...` paths. On macOS, users can also install apps to `~/Applications/` (e.g., `~/Applications/Microsoft Edge.app/...`). This is less common for system browsers but it does happen — meaning the fallback will silently fail for users who installed into their home directory. The existing `findChromeExecutableMac()` (line 511) already handles this by checking both `/Applications/...` and `~/Applications/...` candidates. Consider doing the same here, either by expanding each entry to also check `path.join(os.homedir(), "Applications", ...)` when the `/Applications/...` path does not exist, or by adding home-dir variants to the map. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

What this is trying to fix: macOS LaunchServices can report Edge as `com.microsoft.edgemac`, while the app bundle path lookup can fail because the app's own bundle identity differs. Current `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome.executables.ts` recognizes those LaunchServices IDs in `CHROMIUM_BUNDLE_IDS`, but `detectDefaultChromiumExecutableMac()` still returns `null` as soon as `osascript` or `defaults` fails. After that, `resolveBrowserExecutableForPlatform()` falls into `findChromeExecutableMac()`, whose candidate order can pick Chrome before the user's configured default Edge. So the linked default-browser behavior is still real. This PR is directionally the right fix: keep dynamic `osascript/defaults` resolution first, then use a known bundle-id-to-executable fallback only for recognized Chromium defaults and only when the executable exists. The latest patch also covers `~/Applications`, which addresses the earlier review gap. Blocking before merge: - Needs a regression test in `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome.default-browser.test.ts` for `com.microsoft.edgemac` + failing `osascript`/`defaults` + existing Edge path + existing Chrome fallback path. Expected result must be Edge, not Chrome. - Add the missing-path case too: same bundle ID, no known Edge path, Chrome exists, expected fallback remains Chrome. - Rebase/rerun; current checks are stale/red. I would not close this as obsolete, but I also would not merge it without those tests because the existing Edge test only covers the successful `osascript/defaults` branch. - clawsweeper at 2026-04-29T18:55:40Z: Codex review: needs real behavior proof before merge. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.

Greptile Summary

This PR makes a single-line fix to `src/shared/device-auth-store.test.ts`, changing the seed scope in the "overwrites existing entries for the same normalized role" test from `["operator.read"]` to `["operator.audit"]`. The motivation is clarity: `normalizeDeviceAuthScopes` in `device-auth.ts` expands `"operator.write"` to imply `"operator.read"`. The test asserts the post-overwrite entry has `scopes: ["operator.read", "operator.write"]`. With the old seed of `["operator.read"]`, a reader could incorrectly infer that `"operator.read"` was carried over from the pre-existing entry rather than injected by normalization. Using `["operator.audit"]` — a scope with no implied-expansion rules — makes the complete-overwrite semantics unambiguous. **Key points:** - The change is confined to test seed data; no production code is touched. - The assertions on the expected output were already correct before this PR. - `"operator.audit"` is a valid free-form scope and is correctly discarded on overwrite, which is exactly what the test intends to demonstrate. - No other tests or files are affected.

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge — only test seed data is changed; no production logic is affected. - The PR is a minimal, surgical test-only change. The new seed value (`"operator.audit"`) has no special handling in `normalizeDeviceAuthScopes`, so it is correctly dropped on overwrite, and the expected output already correctly reflects implied-scope expansion. No regressions are possible and the change improves test readability. - No files require special attention. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #60381: browser tool: add force parameter for click and expose evaluate action
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_issue - Number: 60381 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60381 - Title: browser tool: add force parameter for click and expose evaluate action - State: OPEN - Author: Gui-Ni - Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster Body: ```markdown # Browser Tool Enhancement Proposal ## Problem Statement OpenClaw's `browser` tool, built on Playwright, frequently fails to interact with modern frontend frameworks (React/Vue/Svelte) due to strict hit-test and visibility checks during physical click simulation. ### Typical Failure Pattern ``` Error: Element is intercepted - A transparent overlay div is covering the button - The button uses `pointer-events: none` on a parent element - The element has CSS layout that offsets it visually but not in DOM coordinates - Animations/transitions haven't completed before the click is issued ``` This happens because `page.click()` simulates a real physical pointer interaction with full hit-testing, while modern frontend frameworks often build complex DOM structures that look interactive but fail Playwright's strict accessibility/visibility checks. --- ## Proposed Solutions ### Appendix: `javascript:` Goto Hack — Blocked An alternative escape hatch was discovered: using Playwright's `page.goto()` with a `javascript:` URL protocol to inject JS: ```json { "action": "goto", "url": "javascript:document.querySelector('[data-ref=\"e12\"]').click(); void(0);" } ``` **Finding**: ClawX Gateway explicitly rejects this with: ``` GatewayClientRequestError: Navigation blocked: unsupported protocol "javascript:" ``` The Gateway has already anticipated and blocked this vector. This confirms that a proper `evaluate` action must be implemented at the routing level, not workarounded through navigation. --- ## Proposed Solutions ### Solution A: Add `force` Parameter to `click` Action (Low Cost) Playwright natively supports `page.click(selector, { force: true })`, which skips all actionability checks (visibility, stability, animatable, receiving pointer events, etc.). **Implementation**: ```typescript // In browser tool's act handler case "click": const force = params.force === true; await page.click(selector, { force }); break; ``` **When to use**: When the element exists in the DOM but is visually "covered" by transparent layers, or when `pointer-events: none` parent elements block clicks. --- ### Solution B: Add `evaluate` Action (Escape Hatch) Expose Playwright's `page.evaluate()` directly to allow arbitrary JavaScript execution in the page context. **Implementation**: ```typescript case "evaluate": // script: string — JavaScript code to execute in browser context const result = await page.evaluate(params.script); return { success: true, result }; ``` **Capabilities unlocked**: - Direct DOM manipulation to trigger listeners bypassed by physical clicks - Read `window.localStorage` / `sessionStorage` - Extract obfuscated or framework-generated data - Directly call framework methods (e.g., React's `ref.current.click()`) - Bypass CORS restrictions in certain contexts **Example usage**: ```json { "action": "evaluate", "script": "document.querySelector('[data-testid=submit]').click()" } ``` --- ## API Changes ### Extended `act` Action Parameters ```typescript // click subtype { action: "click", ref: "e12", force?: boolean // NEW: skip hit-test and visibility checks } // NEW — evaluate subtype { action: "evaluate", script: string // JavaScript to execute in page context } ``` --- ## Behavior Recommendation | Scenario | Recommended Action | |----------|-------------------| | Simple visible button, no overlays | Default `click` | | Button covered by transparent div | `click` with `force: true` | | Framework-bound event (React `onClick`, Vue `@click`) | `evaluate` with `.click()` | | Need to read `localStorage` / app state | `evaluate` with return statement | | Complex multi-step trigger | `evaluate` with arbitrary JS | --- ## Implementation Pitfalls (暗坑) ### Pitfall 1: `evaluate` Return Value Serialization Playwright's `page.evaluate()` must serialize the return value back to Node.js/Python. **DOM elements cannot be serialized** — attempting to return a raw DOM node will throw or silently return `{}`. | ❌ Wrong | ✅ Correct | |----------|-----------| | `return document.querySelector('.btn')` | `return document.querySelector('.btn').innerText` | | `return window.reactRoot` | `return window.reactRoot.state` | | `return element.getAttribute('href')` | `return element.getAttribute('href')` | **Tool description must include this warning explicitly:** > `The return value of the script must be a primitive type or a JSON-serializable object. Do not return DOM elements or non-serializable objects.` **Implementation**: Add a serialization safety check in the evaluate handler: ```typescript case "evaluate": try { const result = await page.evaluate(params.script); // Verify result is serializable JSON.stringify(result); // will throw if not serializable return { success: true, result }; } catch (e) { return { success: false, error: e.message }; } ``` --- ### Pitfall 2: Model Laziness — `evaluate` Overuse High-intelligence models (GPT-4 class) tend to discover that `evaluate` is a powerful escape hatch and may start using it for **all** interactions instead of attempting normal `click` operations first. This degrades the fidelity of testing (synthetic events behave differently from real user interactions in edge cases). **Consequence**: Interactions that would work fine with `click` might silently fail in production because the model never actually tested the real user flow. **Mitigation — mandatory rule in system prompt:** ``` # 🛡️ Browser Interaction Fallback Rules If your `click` action fails due to "Element is intercepted", "pointer-events: none", or transparent overlays, DO NOT repeat the same click. Immediately adopt one of: 1. **Click the Parent/Child:** Target a structurally adjacent element. The inner ``, ``, or outer wrapper `
` of a button is often not intercepted. 2. **Keyboard Navigation:** If the element is blocked but focusable, click a nearby "safe" area, use `press` action to hit `Tab` until target is focused, then send `Enter` or `Space`. 3. **Scroll to Center:** Intercepted by sticky headers/footers? Attempt to scroll the element to the center of the viewport before clicking. 4. **force: true** (only after the above fail): Use `force: true` as a last-resort retry before falling back to `evaluate`. ``` **The Behavior Recommendation table should be embedded as a hard rule, not a suggestion.** --- ## Risk Assessment ### Security Considerations - `evaluate` allows arbitrary JS execution in the browser context — this is a powerful escape hatch that should be documented as a privileged operation - Suggest marking `evaluate` as a "advanced" or "fallback" action in tool descriptions - The browser profile is already isolated (`browser/openclaw/user-data`), so no cross-session contamination - Serialization validation prevents accidental data leakage through unserializable objects ### Backward Compatibility - Both changes are additive — no existing behavior is modified - `force` defaults to `false` (current behavior unchanged) - `evaluate` is purely new — no conflict with existing actions --- ## Implementation Checklist - [ ] Add `force?: boolean` parameter to click handler - [ ] Add new `evaluate` action case in act handler - [ ] Update tool description/schema to reflect new parameters - [ ] Add documentation in TOOLS.md - [ ] Add test cases for both features --- ## Priority Suggestion **Solution A (`force` parameter)** is trivial to implement and covers the majority of "Element is intercepted" failures. Recommend implementing first. **Solution B (`evaluate` action)** should be added alongside as a proper "escape hatch" for the remaining cases that `force` doesn't solve, and to unlock broader capabilities. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - clawsweeper at 2026-04-28T17:34:05Z: Codex review: keeping this open for maintainer follow-up; there is still a little grit to resolve. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.
How this review workflow works - ClawSweeper keeps one durable marker-backed review comment per issue or PR. - Re-runs edit this comment so the latest verdict, findings, and automation markers stay together instead of adding duplicate bot comments. - A fresh review can be triggered by eligible `@clawsweeper re-review` comments, exact-item GitHub events, scheduled/background review runs, or manual workflow dispatch. - PR/issue authors and users with repository write access can comment `@clawsweeper re-review` or `@clawsweeper re-run` on an open PR or issue to request a fresh review only. - Maintainers can also comment `@clawsweeper review` to request a fresh review only. - Fresh-review commands do not start repair, autofix, rebase, CI repair, or automerge. - Maintainer-only repair and merge flows require explicit commands such as `@clawsweeper autofix`, `@clawsweeper automerge`, `@clawsweeper fix ci`, or `@clawsweeper address review`. - Maintainers can comment `@clawsweeper explain` to ask for more context, or `@clawsweeper stop` to stop active automation.
**Summary** Keep open: current main already has the requested gated `evaluate` action, but the `click` contract still has no opt-in `force` field or forwarding path. The FSB comments are useful prior art for making forced clicks explicit and auditable rather than a default interaction mode. Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection: a click payload with `force: true` has no schema, type, normalizer, CLI flag, existing-session handling, or Playwright forwarding path on current main. I did not run a runtime repro because this review is read-only. **Next step** Queue a focused feature PR because the remaining force-click option is source-reproducible, additive, and bounded to the browser plugin contract and execution path.
Review details Best possible solution: Keep the current gated evaluate implementation, and add explicit opt-in `force?: boolean` click support through the browser action contract, CLI, managed Playwright path, docs/skill guidance, tests, and clear existing-session behavior. Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue? Yes, by source inspection: a click payload with `force: true` has no schema, type, normalizer, CLI flag, existing-session handling, or Playwright forwarding path on current main. I did not run a runtime repro because this review is read-only. Is this the best way to solve the issue? Yes for the remaining work: the narrow maintainable solution is an additive opt-in click field that forwards to Playwright while preserving normal actionability checks by default. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 65574 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/65574 - Title: fix(mcp-stdio): propagate proxy env vars to child processes - State: OPEN - Author: KeWang0622 - Labels: agents, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary MCP stdio child processes silently lose proxy configuration (`HTTPS_PROXY`, `HTTP_PROXY`, `NO_PROXY`) and `NODE_OPTIONS` from the gateway process. This breaks MCP servers in proxy-only environments (sandboxed containers, corporate networks, OpenShell). **Root cause:** The MCP SDK's `StdioClientTransport` intentionally restricts env inheritance to 6 vars on Linux. `resolveStdioMcpServerLaunchConfig` passes user-configured env straight through without compensating for this restriction. **Fix:** Merge proxy-related env vars from `process.env` as lowest-priority defaults before passing to `StdioClientTransport`. User-configured env values always take precedence. When no proxy vars are set, behavior is unchanged (`env` remains `undefined`). ### Changes - **`src/agents/mcp-stdio.ts`**: Added `getProxyEnvDefaults()` to collect proxy env vars from the gateway process. Modified `resolveStdioMcpServerLaunchConfig` to merge these as lowest-priority defaults with user-configured env. - **`src/agents/mcp-stdio.test.ts`**: 9 tests covering proxy propagation, user override precedence, merge behavior, empty-string filtering, and no-op when no proxy vars are set. ### How it works ```js // Proxy vars from gateway (lowest priority) const proxyDefaults = getProxyEnvDefaults(); // User config from openclaw.json (highest priority) const userEnv = toMcpStringRecord(raw.env); // Merge: user wins on conflict const mergedEnv = { ...proxyDefaults, ...userEnv }; ``` ### Prior art Same class of fix as #62878 (Slack Socket Mode proxy support), which was merged for the same reason — child processes not honoring ambient proxy configuration. Fixes #65525 Fixes #65526 ## Test plan - [x] `getProxyEnvDefaults` returns empty object when no proxy vars set - [x] `getProxyEnvDefaults` collects upper and lower case proxy vars - [x] `getProxyEnvDefaults` collects `NO_PROXY` and `NODE_OPTIONS` - [x] `getProxyEnvDefaults` ignores empty string values - [x] Gateway proxy vars propagated when user env not configured - [x] User-configured env overrides gateway proxy vars - [x] Gateway proxy vars merge with user env (user wins on conflict) - [x] `env` is `undefined` when no proxy vars and no user env (no behavior change) - [x] All 9 tests pass: `npx vitest run src/agents/mcp-stdio.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts` - [x] Existing `mcp-transport-config.test.ts` tests still pass - [x] TypeScript compiles cleanly (`tsc --noEmit` — no errors in `mcp-stdio`) - [x] `oxlint` reports 0 warnings, 0 errors ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-12T22:15:03Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes MCP stdio child processes silently losing proxy env vars (`HTTPS_PROXY`, `HTTP_PROXY`, `NO_PROXY` and lowercase variants) and `NODE_OPTIONS` from the gateway because the MCP SDK intentionally restricts env inheritance on Linux. The fix adds `getProxyEnvDefaults()` and merges these as lowest-priority defaults in `resolveStdioMcpServerLaunchConfig`, preserving user-configured env precedence and keeping `env: undefined` when nothing is set. Implementation is correct and well-tested.

Confidence Score: 5/5

Safe to merge — implementation is correct, tests are thorough, and no P0/P1 issues found. The merge logic is correct, user-configured env always wins, and the no-op path (no proxy vars, no user env → undefined) is preserved. The only finding is a P2 note about NODE_OPTIONS with debug flags propagating to child processes, which is the expected OS-level behavior being restored. No files require special attention.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/mcp-stdio.ts Line: 24-32 Comment: **`NODE_OPTIONS` with debug flags propagates to child processes** If the gateway process is launched with `NODE_OPTIONS=--inspect-brk` (common during development), every MCP stdio child process will inherit that flag and pause execution waiting for a debugger to attach — effectively hanging until a debugger connects. `--inspect` is less severe but still causes all children to fight for debug ports. This restores normal OS-level env inheritance (so the behavior matches what a user would see in a plain shell), but it may be surprising in dev environments. Consider filtering out debugger-related `NODE_OPTIONS` tokens, or adding a note in the code/docs that `NODE_OPTIONS` with debug flags will propagate to all MCP children. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````
Reviews (1): Last reviewed commit: ["fix(mcp-stdio): propagate proxy env vars..."](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/9db79968d08a8c529cf45546c4c58662a28de414) | [Re-trigger Greptile](https://app.greptile.com/api/retrigger?id=28147680) - mjamiv at 2026-04-13T01:48:52Z: Filer of #65525 / #65526 here — confirming the fix matches the approach suggested in the issue, and adding a production exposure data point for reviewer context. **Production setup affected:** OpenClaw gateway running inside an OpenShell-managed sandbox in proxy-only mode. The sandbox uses a transparent HTTP CONNECT proxy with TLS interception, and `NODE_OPTIONS=--require ` is exported in the gateway startup script so all `node:tls` traffic trusts the sandbox CA. MCP stdio servers spawned by the gateway never see this `NODE_OPTIONS` (or `HTTPS_PROXY`/`HTTP_PROXY`), so they fail TLS handshake to upstream APIs immediately on first request. **Why this PR is the right shape:** - The root cause sits in the MCP SDK's `StdioClientTransport` `DEFAULT_INHERITED_ENV_VARS` allowlist (sudo-style 6-var restriction on Linux), so the fix must compensate one layer up in `resolveStdioMcpServerLaunchConfig`. Patching the SDK directly would require a fork. - Lowest-priority merge (`{ ...proxyDefaults, ...userEnv }`) is correct — it preserves the existing escape hatch where operators set `env: { HTTPS_PROXY: "" }` per-server to opt out, and it never overrides explicit user config. - The `--inspect*` stripping in `getProxyEnvDefaults()` is a nice extra: child processes inheriting `NODE_OPTIONS=--inspect` from a debugged gateway would otherwise crash on port collisions. Worth calling out in the changelog. - The case-deduplication tests (lowercase wins when both set, empty lowercase disables uppercase) match the convention in `src/infra/net/proxy-env.ts`, so this stays consistent with how the rest of the codebase resolves proxy state. **Same class of fix as #62878** (Slack Socket Mode HTTPS_PROXY support, merged in 2026.4.8) — that one was the same shape: gateway has proxy env, child process / subsystem doesn't see it, fix merges proxy env at the launch boundary. The prior-art reference in the PR body is accurate. The current local workaround is to manually duplicate `NODE_OPTIONS` + `HTTPS_PROXY` into every MCP server's `env` block in `openclaw.json`, which is brittle (every new MCP server needs the same boilerplate, and the failure mode is silent — TLS error from inside the child, not a config validation error at gateway startup). This PR removes that footgun. Happy to test the fix on the soak environment once it lands in a beta. - KeWang0622 at 2026-04-13T02:25:31Z: Thanks @mjamiv — great to have production confirmation from the original reporter. The per-server `env` boilerplate workaround you describe is exactly the footgun this PR eliminates. The `--inspect*` stripping and case-dedup behaviors have been through several rounds of bot review and are now tested (22 cases). Happy to address any further feedback from the maintainers. - clawsweeper at 2026-04-27T02:30:04Z: Codex review: needs real behavior proof before merge. **Workflow note:** Future ClawSweeper reviews update this same comment in place.

**Summary** The PR adds MCP stdio launch-config logic and tests to propagate gateway proxy env vars plus sanitized NODE_OPTIONS into MCP child process env defaults. Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection. github_context:

- Title: test: add integration test for table-aware block streaming chunking
- State: OPEN
- Author: zongqichen
- Labels: P3, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

#66568 added table-aware splitting to the block chunker (`EmbeddedBlockChunker`) and `chunkByParagraph`, preventing markdown tables from being split across messages during block streaming. The fix includes unit tests but no integration-level coverage through the `subscribeEmbeddedPiSession` pipeline.

The existing fence-awareness integration test in `pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.streams-soft-chunks-paragraph-preference.test.ts` ("avoids splitting inside fenced code blocks") is the direct analog — a matching "avoids splitting inside markdown tables" case should be added for parity.

## Suggested test

Add a case to the `streams-soft-chunks-paragraph-preference` test file (or a sibling file) using the existing `createParagraphChunkedBlockReplyHarness`:

- Input: `"Intro\n\n| A | B |\n|---|---|\n| 1 | 2 |\n\nOutro"`
- Expected: 3 block replies — `"Intro"`, the full table as one chunk, `"Outro"`
- Verify the table is never split across `onBlockReply` calls

## Context

- PR: #66568
- Test harness: `pi-embedded-subscribe.e2e-harness.ts`
- Reference case: "avoids splitting inside fenced code blocks" in the same file
```

github_context:
GitHub item:

- Type: github_issue
- Number: 72111
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/72111
- Title: MCP streamable-http transport — spawned server process times out
- State: OPEN
- Author: shao-zhijie
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Issue: MCP streamable-http transport — spawned server process times out

**OpenClaw version:** 2026.4.24  
**MCP SDK version:** @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.29.0  
**OpenSpace version:** 1.26.0 (HKUDS/OpenSpace)

---

### Problem

When configuring an MCP server with `transport: "streamable-http"`, OpenClaw spawns the server process correctly but the connection **times out after 30000ms**:

```
failed to start server "openspace" (/opt/homebrew/bin/openspace-mcp --transport streamable-http --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8083): Error: MCP server connection timed out after 30000ms
```

The server process does start (verified by `lsof` showing port 8083 in LISTEN state), but OpenClaw's MCP client cannot establish a connection to it.

### What works

- **STDIO transport**: works correctly
- **Accept header**: fixed (no more 406 errors — issue #66940 appears resolved)
- **Direct curl to OpenSpace HTTP endpoint**: works correctly with correct Accept header:
  ```bash
  curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8083/mcp \
    -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
    -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize",...}'
  # Returns valid response
  ```
- **Session persistence**: OpenSpace HTTP correctly returns `mcp-session-id` and a second request with the session ID also works

### Configuration

```json
{
  "openspace": {
    "command": "/opt/homebrew/bin/openspace-mcp",
    "args": ["--transport", "streamable-http", "--host", "127.0.0.1", "--port", "8083"],
    "transport": "streamable-http",
    "url": "http://127.0.0.1:8083/mcp",
    "env": { ... }
  }
}
```

### Hypothesis

When OpenClaw spawns a child process for `streamable-http` transport, it may not be properly handling the process lifecycle or the stdio redirect. The spawned process starts and binds to the port, but the MCP client transport fails to complete the connection handshake.

The issue appears to be in how OpenClaw's `StreamableHTTPClientTransport` interacts with a **spawned child process** (vs a pre-existing HTTP server). The Accept header fix in #66940 resolved the pre-existing server case, but the **process spawning** case may have a separate issue.

### Environment

- macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64)
- Node.js v25.7.0
- Python 3.14 (OpenSpace MCP server)
- Launchd-managed process spawning vs OpenClaw-managed spawning

---

**Labels:** mcp, streamable-http, transport
```

- State: OPEN

- Labels: size: XS, extensions: acpx, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient

Body:
```markdown
test(acpx): mirror resolver's path construction in MCP server test helper

The ACPx config test expected source-mode (tsx loader) paths for built-in MCP servers, but the resolver correctly prefers dist entries when they exist. Update the test helper to match this runtime behavior.

- Mirror resolver's path construction: compute openClawRoot from resolveAcpxPluginRoot, then use path.join/path.relative to build the correct absolute dist path.
- Remove the broken entrypoint.replace("/src/", "/dist/") which never matched because the test passes "src/mcp/..." without a leading separator.

Fixes #80431

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The test helper `expectedMcpServerArgs` used `entrypoint.replace("/src/", "/dist/")` which never matched because the caller passes "src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.ts" (no leading `/`). This caused the dist-existence check to silently check the wrong path (`src/mcp/*.js` instead of `dist/mcp/*.js`) and always fall back to source-mode args, even when `dist/` existed.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Ubuntu 22.04, Node 20, pnpm 9. Built dist exists for built-in MCP servers under `dist/mcp/`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```
cd /tmp/openclaw-fork
git checkout fix/issue-80431-acpx-dist-path
pnpm build
ls -la dist/mcp/  # confirm dist exists
npx vitest run extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**
Terminal output showing the corrected test helper now constructs the correct absolute dist path:
```
$ npx vitest run extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
 PASS  extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts
   ✓ injects the built-in plugin-tools MCP server only when explicitly enabled
   ✓ injects the built-in openclaw-tools MCP server only when explicitly enabled
```

Before the fix, the helper computed `distEntry` as `src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.js` (because `/src/` did not match) and `fs.existsSync` returned false, so the test always fell back to source-mode args even when `dist/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.js` existed.

**Observed result after fix:** The helper now computes the same absolute dist path as the production resolver (`resolvePluginToolsMcpServerConfig`), correctly returning `[distEntry]` when `dist/` is present and falling back to `["--import", tsx, sourceEntry]` otherwise.

**What was not tested:** No runtime resolver logic changed; only the test helper was updated to mirror the resolver's path construction.
```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Adds one small personal-agent QA case for failure recovery.

The new scenario checks the part that matters after something goes wrong: the agent keeps completed and failed work separate, writes a local recovery artifact, gives the next step, and does not claim the blocked external step was retried or finished.

This stays inside QA-Lab. No runtime behavior changes.

## Why

The personal-agent pack already covers followthrough and proof-backed completion claims. This adds the adjacent reliability check: when a workflow is only partly done, the agent should say that clearly and stop at the retry boundary instead of smoothing it over.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
A personal-agent QA turn should report partial failure honestly, preserve the retry boundary, and write a local recovery artifact before replying.

Real environment tested:
Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `feature/personal-failure-recovery`, using QA-Lab with `mock-openai` and the local qa-channel/gateway harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/scenario-packs.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/scenario-catalog.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts -t "personal-agent pack"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.test.ts -t "personal failure recovery"
OPENCLAW_ENABLE_PRIVATE_QA_CLI=1 node openclaw.mjs qa suite --provider-mode mock-openai --scenario personal-failure-recovery --concurrency 1
OPENCLAW_ENABLE_PRIVATE_QA_CLI=1 node openclaw.mjs qa suite --provider-mode mock-openai --pack personal-agent --concurrency 1
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm tsgo:test:extensions
pnpm format:docs:check
pnpm docs:check-mdx
git diff --check
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/qa-lab/src/scenario-packs.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/scenario-packs.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.test.ts

github_context:

- Type: github_pr

- State: CLOSED
- Author: xx205
- Labels: size: S, proof: supplied, proof: sufficient, P2, rating: 🐚 platinum hermit, status: 👀 ready for maintainer look

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
- forward `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` into browser screenshot tool results
- forward the same image sanitization limits into `snapshot --labels` image results
- add regression coverage for both browser image-returning paths

## Problem
`browser screenshot` normalized images to the browser layer's `<= 2000px` limit first, but then returned them through `imageResultFromFile()` without passing runtime image sanitization config.

That meant the final tool image still fell back to the default `1200px` sanitize limit, even when `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` was configured higher. The same omission existed for `browser snapshot --labels`.

## Fix
Thread runtime image sanitization limits into both browser image-returning call sites:
- `browser:screenshot`
- `browser:snapshot` when `labels=true`

This keeps browser tool image behavior aligned with the documented global image sanitization setting and with other OpenClaw image paths that already forward this config.

## Verification
- focused browser extension test suite passed:
  - `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts` (`51 passed`)
- direct runtime check with the same input image showed:
  - default config: final browser screenshot output still reduced to `1200x625`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 1920`: final output became `1920x1000`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 2000`: final output stayed at `2000x1042`
  - `imageMaxDimensionPx = 3000`: final output still stayed at `2000x1042`, as expected because browser pre-normalization already caps screenshots at `2000px`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: browser screenshot and `browser snapshot --labels` image results ignored `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` on the final tool-image sanitize step, so a browser screenshot could still come out at `1200px` even when the config was set higher.

**Real environment tested**: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS using the real browser screenshot normalization path plus the browser tool image-result path with a local PNG test image as the input.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran the browser screenshot runtime check against the patched branch, varying `agents.defaults.imageMaxDimensionPx` across default, `1920`, `2000`, and `3000`, then inspected the generated image metadata.

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
live output from runtime image metadata inspection:
normalized browser layer: 2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
default final output:    1200x625,  107491 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 1920:         1920x1000, 214162 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 2000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
configured 3000:         2000x1042, 246537 bytes, image/jpeg
```

**Observed result after fix**: after the patch, browser-originated image results now honor the configured sanitize limit up to the browser layer's own `2000px` ceiling. `1920` produces `1920x1000`, `2000` produces `2000x1042`, and `3000` does not exceed `2000x1042` because browser pre-normalization already caps that path.

**What was not tested**: a full remote provider call was not run; this proof covers the local browser screenshot normalization path and the actual browser tool image packaging and sanitize path.

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #39248: Bug: sandbox.mode: "non-main" silently breaks sessions_spawn subagent initialization
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 39248
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/39248
- Title: Bug: sandbox.mode: "non-main" silently breaks sessions_spawn subagent initialization
- State: OPEN
- Author: alexputici
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode` is set to `"non-main"`, sub-agents spawned via `sessions_spawn` fail to initialize. Sessions are accepted (return valid `childSessionKey` and `runId`) but never start executing — zero tokens, zero messages, no transcript files created. **No errors appear in gateway logs.**

## Environment

- OpenClaw Version: 2026.3.2 (85377a2)
- OS: macOS 15.3.0 (Apple Silicon, arm64)
- Node: v25.8.0
- Docker Desktop: running (coding agent sandbox works fine)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode: "non-main"` in config
2. Restart gateway
3. From main agent, spawn a sub-agent:
   ```
   sessions_spawn(task="Reply with: test", model="sonnet")
   ```
4. Check `sessions_history` or `subagents list` after 30+ seconds

## Expected Behavior

Sub-agent starts within seconds, `systemSent: true`, tokens accumulate, task completes.

## Actual Behavior

- `sessions_spawn` returns `status: "accepted"` with valid session key
- Sub-agent appears in `subagents list` as `running`
- `sessions_history` returns `messages: []` indefinitely
- No transcript file created on disk
- `systemSent` never set to `true`
- `totalTokens` never set
- **Zero error output in gateway logs**
- Eventually times out via `runTimeoutSeconds`

## Bisect Results

Systematically tested with a simple one-line task (`Reply with exactly: ...`):

| Test | sandbox.mode | Docker settings | Result |
|------|-------------|----------------|--------|
| 1 | non-main | network: bridge, readOnlyRoot: true | stall |
| 2 | non-main | network: bridge, readOnlyRoot: false | stall |
| 3 | **off** | (same as test 2) | **works (4s)** |
| 4 | non-main | minimal (image + workdir only) | stall |

The issue is **not** caused by specific Docker settings (network, readOnlyRoot, tmpfs). Any `sandbox.mode` that enables Docker for subagents causes silent failure.

## Notes

- **Cron isolated sessions work fine** with `sandbox.mode: "non-main"` on the same config — they appear to use a different code path that bypasses container initialization.
- **Per-agent sandbox overrides are unaffected**: a coding agent with `sandbox.mode: "all"` and its own Docker settings works correctly.
- Possibly related to #5813 (similar symptoms, different attributed cause).
- The silent failure with zero log output makes this very difficult to diagnose.

## Workaround

Set `agents.defaults.sandbox.mode: "off"` and use per-agent `sandbox.mode: "all"` overrides for agents that specifically need sandboxing (e.g., coding agents with `tools.exec.host: "sandbox"`).
```

**Recommended Action**: Verify sandbox mode initialization path and add integration tests.

Best possible solution:

Keep `non-main` sandboxing supported and make Docker-backed native child runs either reach prompt/transcript startup or transition to a terminal failed state with actionable sandbox startup diagnostics and regression coverage.

Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue?

Yes at source level, but not as a live Docker-backed current-main repro in this review. Current `main` still creates generated child session keys, classifies them as sandboxed in `non-main` mode, and acknowledges the run before child sandbox startup executes.

Is this the best way to solve the issue?

Not applicable as a patch review. The maintainable fix should stay in the native subagent startup/lifecycle path and should not disable `non-main` sandboxing or rely only on generic Docker diagnostics.

- `P1`: A supported core `sessions_spawn` workflow can leave spawned subagents accepted but inert, blocking multi-agent workflows for affected users.

- `impact:crash-loop`: The issue is about a hang/availability failure where spawned runs remain stuck until timeout instead of starting or failing cleanly.

Greptile Summary

This PR fixes the root cause of #42072 by introducing `loadInternalHooksForStartup`, a startup-specific loader that registers workspace-local hooks for every configured agent workspace (not just the default) and wraps each workspace handler with a scope guard so hooks only fire for events belonging to that workspace. If an unexpected error escapes the inner per-entry error handling during shared hook loading, the entire workspace-local loading loop is silently skipped — the exact regression this PR is meant to prevent. - `createSharedHookHandler` bypasses the workspace-override suppression for all `gateway:startup` events, meaning a workspace-local startup hook with the same name as a shared one does not replace it — both fire. This is inconsistent with non-startup event behaviour and is not covered by the test suite. - `server-startup.ts` change is minimal and correct; `normalizeWorkspaceDirs` correctly deduplicates the default workspace dir that appears in both the `defaultWorkspaceDir` argument and the `listAgentWorkspaceDirs` result.

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 44143 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/44143 - Title: fix: serialize outbound deliveries per channel+recipient - State: OPEN - Author: nicolasgrasset - Labels: channel: mattermost, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary When multiple sessions (main + sub-agent) send messages to the same recipient concurrently, replies can arrive out of order because each session's delivery call races independently on the channel's HTTP endpoint. Users see jumbled message sequences — especially noticeable on Telegram and Discord where ordering matters for readability. **Reproduction:** Run a main agent and sub-agent that both reply to the same user within a short window. Without this fix, the second session's message frequently lands before the first's, depending on network timing. **Fix:** Introduces a `DeliverySerializer` that chains concurrent deliveries to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` key in FIFO order while keeping different recipients fully concurrent. The serializer is in-memory only (runtime ordering, not persistence) and auto-cleans keys when queues drain. The existing write-ahead delivery queue for crash recovery is unaffected. A follow-up commit wraps `fn()` in `Promise.resolve().then()` so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejection rather than hanging the result promise forever (addresses review feedback). Includes tests for ordering, concurrency across targets, error propagation, sync-throw handling, and queue cleanup. Rebased to 2026.3.13. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-12T14:55:52Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR introduces a `DeliverySerializer` — an in-memory per-key async mutex — to guarantee FIFO ordering of outbound messages sent concurrently to the same `channel:accountId:recipient` target, fixing a race condition where sub-agent and main-session deliveries could arrive out of order. - The Promise-chain approach in `DeliverySerializer` is sound: FIFO ordering, error isolation (a failed delivery never blocks subsequent ones), and automatic Map cleanup are all correctly implemented and well-tested. - The integration in `deliver.ts` is minimal and preserves all existing error-handling, partial-failure tracking, and write-ahead queue ACK/NACK logic. - The five-case test suite is deterministic and covers ordering, concurrency, error propagation, cleanup, and stress scenarios. - One latent robustness gap: if `fn()` ever throws synchronously, `resultPromise` will never settle (hanging the caller permanently) and the stored `tail` will produce an unhandled rejection. This cannot happen with the current `async` call-site but is worth guarding against defensively with `Promise.resolve().then(() => fn())`.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- This PR is safe to merge; the mutex logic is correct and the integration change is minimal with no risk to existing error-handling paths. - The Promise-chain mutex pattern is well-understood and correctly implemented. FIFO ordering, error isolation, and cleanup are all verified by tests. The only deduction is for the defensive-programming gap where a synchronously-throwing `fn()` would cause an eternal hang and unhandled rejection — low risk in practice since the call-site is always async, but worth hardening before reuse. - src/infra/outbound/delivery-serializer.ts — review the synchronous-throw robustness concern in the `serialize` method.
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/infra/outbound/delivery-serializer.ts Line: 25-44 Comment: **Synchronous throw from `fn()` causes `resultPromise` to hang forever** If `fn()` throws synchronously (before returning a Promise), the arrow callback inside `prev.then(...)` throws, which causes `tail` to reject. Because neither `resolve` nor `reject` is ever called, `resultPromise` never settles — so `await resultPromise` in this same `serialize` call hangs indefinitely, leaking that Map entry forever. Additionally, the rejecting `tail` stored in the Map has no rejection handler attached, which would produce an unhandled promise rejection warning. In the current call-site `() => deliverOutboundPayloadsCore(wrappedParams)` this can't happen because `deliverOutboundPayloadsCore` is `async`. But a small defensive guard makes the serializer safe for any caller: ```ts // Before storing tail, ensure fn() is always called asynchronously // so a synchronous throw is converted to a rejected Promise and // resolve/reject are always reachable. const runFn = () => Promise.resolve() .then(() => fn()) .then( (v) => { resolve(v); }, (e) => { reject(e); }, ); const tail: Promise = prev.then(runFn, runFn); ``` This ensures `resultPromise` always settles and `tail` always resolves. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

Serialized sends always arrive in FIFO order; raw concurrent sends arrive scrambled. All 6 delivery-serializer tests pass locally, plus the full 220-test suite. - nicolasgrasset at 2026-03-12T20:40:55Z: Rebased this branch onto the latest main (2026.3.11) and force-pushed the updated branch. This PR fixes a bug where the OpenResponses SSE stream for tool-call responses was incorrectly emitting a `response.completed` terminal event instead of `response.incomplete`. Greptile Summary

This PR bundles two bug fixes: (1) it detects `tool_use`/`tool_result` history-corruption errors from the Anthropic API and surfaces a friendly recovery message directing users to `/new`, and (2) it ensures the i18n locale is fully loaded before the first render of the Lit application component. **Key changes:** - `errors.ts`: Adds `isToolUseResultMismatchError` / `TOOL_USE_RESULT_MISMATCH_RE` and wires it into both `formatAssistantErrorText` and `sanitizeUserFacingText` (behind the existing `errorContext` guard). - `translate.ts`: Makes `loadLocale` `async` and exposes `i18n.ready: Promise` so callers can await initial locale resolution. - `app.ts`: Introduces a `localeReady` state flag; the component renders `nothing` until both `i18n.ready` and any explicit `setLocale` call from app settings have settled, preventing a flash of untranslated UI. - Tests are added or updated for all three areas. **Issues found:** - The first alternative of `TOOL_USE_RESULT_MISMATCH_RE` is just `tool_use_id`, which matches any string containing that substring. Unrelated validation errors (e.g. `"Invalid format for tool_use_id"`, `"tool_use_id is required"`) will incorrectly display `"Conversation history corruption detected"` — misleading advice that may prompt users to discard their session unnecessarily. - In `app.ts`, `i18n.ready` (reads from `localStorage`/`navigator`) and `settingsLocale` (reads from app settings) are run concurrently via `Promise.all`. If the two locale sources disagree, the non-deterministic resolution order of `setLocale` means the final rendered locale could come from either source.

Confidence Score: 3/5

- Mostly safe to merge; two non-critical issues should be addressed before landing. - The i18n fix is clean and the test improvement is strictly better. The tool-mismatch fix solves a real user-facing problem. However, the overly broad `tool_use_id` regex alternative can cause unrelated validation errors to display an incorrect "history corruption" message, and the concurrent `Promise.all` locale-loading pattern introduces a low-probability but silent race condition when `localStorage` and app settings disagree on the active locale. - `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` (regex breadth) and `ui/src/ui/app.ts` (concurrent locale loading).
Prompt To Fix All With AI `````markdown This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts Line: 838-839 Comment: **Overly broad `tool_use_id` alternative in regex** The first branch of the regex — `tool_use_id` — matches any string that merely contains the substring `"tool_use_id"`, with no further constraints. This means error messages completely unrelated to a history-corruption mismatch (e.g. `"Invalid format for tool_use_id"`, `"tool_use_id is required"`, or `"Unsupported tool_use_id type"`) will all return `"Conversation history corruption detected. Please use /new to start a fresh session."`, which is incorrect advice and will confuse users. The other two alternatives (`tool_result.*corresponding.*tool_use` and `unexpected.*tool.*block`) are adequately specific. The first alternative should be tightened to require additional context, for example: ```suggestion const TOOL_USE_RESULT_MISMATCH_RE = /tool_result.*tool_use_id.*not found|tool_result.*corresponding.*tool_use|unexpected.*tool.*block/i; ``` This matches the actual Anthropic API error strings exercised by the tests (`"tool_result block with tool_use_id … not found in the immediately preceding assistant message"`) while avoiding false positives on generic validation errors that happen to reference the field name. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: ui/src/ui/app.ts Line: 119-133 Comment: **Potential race condition between `i18n.ready` and `settingsLocale`** Both `i18n.ready` (which loads the locale persisted in `localStorage` / derived from `navigator.language`) and `settingsLocale` (which loads the locale from app `settings`) are kicked off concurrently via `Promise.all`. Because `setLocale` is not re-entrant, whichever resolves *last* wins and sets `this.locale`. If the two sources disagree — e.g. `localStorage` says `"zh-CN"` and `settings.locale` says `"fr"` — the final rendered locale will be whichever translation finishes loading later, which is non-deterministic. App settings should be the authoritative source, so `settingsLocale` ought to be applied *after* `i18n.ready` resolves to avoid being overwritten: ``` i18n.ready .then(() => isSupportedLocale(this.settings.locale) ? i18n.setLocale(this.settings.locale) : Promise.resolve()) .then(() => { this.localeReady = true; }, () => { this.localeReady = true; }); ``` This is a low-probability edge case (requires the two stores to diverge), but it would be an invisible, intermittent bug. How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #46753: Cap global cache maps to prevent memory leaks in long-running processes
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 46753 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/46753 - Title: Cap global cache maps to prevent memory leaks in long-running processes - State: OPEN - Author: kevinWangSheng - Labels: agents, size: XL, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 auth-provider, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Problem: Several global `Map` caches in the agent runtime grow indefinitely — they never evict entries. In long-running gateway/daemon processes, this is a slow memory leak. - Why it matters: The gateway can run for days. Caches like `workspaceFileCache` store full file contents (up to 2MB each) and never clean up, eventually pressuring the heap. - What changed: Added max-size caps with oldest-first eviction to four caches: - `bootstrap-cache.ts`: max 64 entries - `workspace.ts` (`workspaceFileCache`): max 128 entries - `cache-trace.ts` (`writers`): max 32 entries - `context.ts` (`MODEL_CACHE`): max 256 entries - What did NOT change: Cache semantics are identical. Eviction only kicks in at counts well above normal operation. No TTL or LRU tracking — just a simple size cap. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Gateway / orchestration ## User-visible / Behavior Changes None — caps are passive and only trigger at abnormally high entry counts. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No ## Repro + Verification ### Steps 1. `pnpm build` — clean 2. `pnpm test` — 17105 passed, all failures pre-existing 3. `pnpm format` — clean ### Expected Build and tests unaffected. ### Actual Confirmed. ## Evidence - [x] `pnpm build` clean - [x] `pnpm test` same pass/fail as main ## Human Verification (required) - Verified: build, test suite, format - Edge cases: cap values chosen to be well above typical counts (a workspace has ~9 bootstrap files, not 64; model registry has ~30 models, not 256) - Not verified: long-running daemon to observe eviction in production ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? No - Migration needed? No ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - Revert this PR — no config to restore - Symptom to watch: cache misses causing extra disk reads after eviction (performance, not correctness) ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Evicting a still-needed cache entry causes a redundant disk read. - Mitigation: Caps are 5-10x above normal counts. The evicted entry will just be re-read on next access. ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-15T02:43:17Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR addresses a legitimate memory-leak risk by adding oldest-first eviction caps to four global `Map` caches (`bootstrap-cache`, `workspaceFileCache`, `writers`, `MODEL_CACHE`) and bundles a large batch of tool-schema hardening changes (descriptions, `minimum`/`maximum`, `maxItems`) across 13 tool files. The cache-capping logic is correct and the `QueuedFileWriter` eviction in `cache-trace.ts` is safe because the underlying writer uses `fs.appendFile` (pending writes complete and append-only semantics prevent corruption). Key findings: - **`durationMs` minimum is a breaking change** (`nodes-tool.ts`): the field previously had no lower bound; adding `minimum: 1000` silently rejects valid sub-second durations. The PR description incorrectly states there are no behavior changes. - **Forward reference in `context.ts`**: `MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES` is used in `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` but declared 36 lines later — valid TypeScript, but confusing to read top-to-bottom. - **Cap inconsistency in `context.ts`**: `applyConfiguredContextWindows` writes to the same `MODEL_CACHE` without checking `MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES`, so a large user config could push the map past the cap and trigger eviction of configured entries on the next `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` call. Likely benign in practice but worth a comment.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Safe to merge with minor caveats — the cache-capping logic is correct, but one schema change is a quiet breaking change and the MODEL_CACHE cap has an inconsistency worth fixing. - The core cache-eviction changes are sound: eviction is called synchronously before every insert, the QueuedFileWriter eviction is safe due to append-only semantics, and the chosen caps are well above real-world counts. The schema hardening is broadly positive. Score deducted one point for the undocumented breaking change on `durationMs` (new minimum: 1000) and the inconsistency in `context.ts` where `applyConfiguredContextWindows` bypasses the same cap that `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` enforces. - src/agents/tools/nodes-tool.ts (durationMs minimum change) and src/agents/context.ts (MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES placement and cap inconsistency)

Comments Outside Diff (3)

 1. `src/agents/context.ts`, line 47 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/47136a04fc45c1362f09014cad06e1b25dd36240/src/agents/context.ts#L47)) **`MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES` referenced before declaration** `MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES` is used here (line 47) but declared 36 lines later at line 83. TypeScript resolves this correctly at runtime because the function body is only executed after module initialization, but the inverted ordering is non-obvious to readers: seeing an undefined-looking constant used inside a function before the reader ever sees its declaration can be confusing. Consider moving the constant declaration (and the companion `MODEL_CACHE` declaration) before `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` so the file reads top-to-bottom without forward references. 2. `src/agents/tools/nodes-tool.ts`, line 651-657 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/47136a04fc45c1362f09014cad06e1b25dd36240/src/agents/tools/nodes-tool.ts#L651-L657)) **`minimum: 1000` is a breaking validation change for `durationMs`** The existing schema constraint for `durationMs` was only `{ maximum: 300_000 }`, which allowed values from 0 up to 5 minutes. This PR adds `minimum: 1000`, silently rejecting any `durationMs` between 0–999 ms. A short sub-second clip (e.g. `durationMs: 500`) is a reasonable use case that would now fail schema validation — previously it was accepted. The PR description states "Cache semantics are identical" and "behavior changes: None", but this is a tightening of the validation surface that could break existing agent workflows silently. If the intent is truly to disallow sub-second recordings, the description should note this as a behavioral change. If not, the `minimum` should be lowered (e.g. `minimum: 100`) or removed entirely to preserve backward compatibility. 3. `src/agents/context.ts`, line 58-79 ([link](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/47136a04fc45c1362f09014cad06e1b25dd36240/src/agents/context.ts#L58-L79)) **`applyConfiguredContextWindows` bypasses the cache cap** `applyDiscoveredContextWindows` carefully checks `MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES` before inserting, but `applyConfiguredContextWindows` calls `params.cache.set` unconditionally (line 77). Both functions write into the same `MODEL_CACHE` map. If a user configures more than 256 models in their config file, `applyConfiguredContextWindows` would silently push the cache past the cap. On the next call to `applyDiscoveredContextWindows`, the eviction would then remove entries that were written by the configured path — the opposite of what you'd want (configured entries should be sticky). This is probably fine for the stated use-case ("model registry has ~30 models, not 256"), but the inconsistency is worth documenting with a comment, or the configured-models path should also apply the same guard.

Path: src/agents/context.ts Line: 47 Comment: **`MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES` referenced before declaration** `MAX_MODEL_CACHE_ENTRIES` is used here (line 47) but declared 36 lines later at line 83. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: github_pr - Number: 48635 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48635 - Title: feat(usage): add model + thinking level to usage footer + responseUsageDefault config - State: OPEN - Author: nusquama - Labels: size: XS, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, status: 📣 needs proof Body: ```markdown ## Problem Two related gaps in the `/usage` footer: 1. The footer (`/usage tokens` or `/usage full`) shows token counts and cost but not **which model** or **thinking level** was used for that response. In multi-agent or multi-model setups, knowing the model at a glance is operationally important. 2. `responseUsage` is per-session only (set via `/usage` slash command). It resets on every session reset. There is no way to configure a default in config, so fleet operators must re-run `/usage full` in every new session. Session-level `/usage` overrides still take precedence. ## Backwards compatibility - All new params are optional — existing callers are unaffected - `responseUsageDefault` defaults to `undefined` (falls back to existing `"off"` behavior) - No breaking changes ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T01:57:11Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR extends `extractToolCallContent()` in the ACP event mapper to forward `image` content blocks from tool results to ACP clients (e.g., Feishu, Discord), in addition to the previously-supported `text` blocks. The change is minimal and well-tested. **Key changes:** - Added an `else if` branch in `extractToolCallContent()` that handles `type: "image"` blocks, validating that both `data` and `mimeType` are strings before forwarding them as `ImageContent`. - Added comprehensive unit tests covering text-only, image-only, mixed, malformed (missing fields), empty content, and string-fallback cases. **Issue found:** - The image-block validation checks `typeof entry.data === "string"` but doesn't guard against an empty string (`""`), unlike the text-block branch which uses `.trim()`. An image block with `data: ""` or `mimeType: ""` would be forwarded to clients as a valid image, likely causing rendering failures. The test suite also doesn't cover this edge case.

Confidence Score: 5/5

Safe to merge; the only finding is a minor edge-case validation gap that won't be triggered in normal usage. The implementation is correct for all realistic inputs. The single P2 finding requires a malformed tool result (empty-string `data` or `mimeType`) to trigger, which does not occur in normal tool output. Tests are thorough for the documented cases. No breaking changes or protocol modifications needed. No files require special attention beyond the minor validation gap in `src/acp/event-mapper.ts`.

Logic is correct for the happy path but allows empty-string `data`/`mimeType` values through, unlike the text-block branch which guards against empty strings. | | src/acp/event-mapper.test.ts | New unit tests cover text, image, mixed, malformed (missing data/mimeType), empty content, and string-fallback cases. target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #58135: [Feature]: expose promptMode parameter in sessions_spawn for deterministic blank sub-agents
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: github_issue
- Number: 58135
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58135
- Title: [Feature]: expose promptMode parameter in sessions_spawn for deterministic blank sub-agents
- State: OPEN
- Author: esteban-dozsa
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

When spawning sub-agents via sessions_spawn, there is no way to control the promptMode used for the child agent run. Sub-agents always default to minimal prompt mode, which injects AGENTS.md and TOOLS.md into the system prompt.

### Problem to solve

The PromptMode `none` mode is exactly what's needed for deterministic blank sub-agents — specialist workers that run with no inherited persona, no memory, and no workspace context. This is critical for use cases like:

• Contract review against a fixed playbook (no ambient context leaking in)
• Document analysis with strictly controlled input/output
• Any workflow where reproducibility requires eliminating inherited state


### Proposed solution

### The OpenClaw codebase already supports three prompt modes internally (system-prompt.ts):

• full — all sections (default for main sessions)
• minimal — omits Skills, Memory Recall, Self-Update, Reply Tags, Heartbeats (default for sub-agents)
• none — returns only the base identity line, no workspace files injected

Add promptMode as an optional parameter to sessions_spawn:
```
// In the spawn handler parameter schema, add:
promptMode?: "full" | "minimal" | "none"

// Forward to the child run config
The parameter should:
```
• Accept "full" | "minimal" | "none" (default: current behavior — minimal for sub-agents)
• Be forwarded to the child session's system prompt builder
• Be documented alongside existing spawn parameters (task, model, thinking, sandbox, etc.)

Expected Behavior
```
await sessions_spawn({
  task: "Review this contract against the attached playbook",
  promptMode: "none",       // no workspace files, identity line only
  sandbox: "require",       // filesystem isolation
  model: "anthropic/claude-haiku-3.5",
  attachments: [{ name: "contract.pdf", content: "...", encoding: "base64" }],
  cleanup: "delete",
});
```
### This spawns a sub-agent with:

• No AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, MEMORY.md, TOOLS.md injected
• No Skills, Memory Recall, or Self-Update sections
• Only the base identity line + the provided attachments
• Deterministic, reproducible behavior across runs


### Alternatives considered

### Current Workarounds and Why They're Insufficient

1. Empty workspace directory — Setting agentId to a blank workspace removes the injected files, but the minimal prompt mode still runs the injection code path and produces section headers in the system prompt. Not truly blank.
2. Plugin with custom tool — A plugin could register a tool that wraps sessions_spawn, but since promptMode isn't a parameter, the plugin can't force none mode through the public API.
3. Importing internal APIs — A plugin could bypass the tool surface and call internal spawn APIs directly where promptMode exists. This works but is fragile across versions.

### Impact

No true isolated sub-agent can be spwaned.

### Evidence/examples

_No response_

### Additional information

• Low risk — promptMode already exists internally; this just exposes it through the spawn API
• No breaking changes — parameter is optional, defaults preserve current behavior
• Consistent with existing architecture — mirrors how model and thinking override defaults in spawn

Body:
```markdown
# Feature Request: Per-agent default model and reasoning_effort in ACPX plugin config

## Problem

When OpenClaw spawns ACP sessions via `sessions_spawn` or `/acp spawn`, there is no way to configure default `model` or `reasoning_effort` per agent harness. Each new session starts with the agent's built-in default model, requiring manual post-spawn configuration via `/acp model` and `/acp set reasoning_effort` every time.

This is particularly painful for teams running multiple harnesses (e.g., Codex + Claude Code + Gemini CLI) where each needs a different model and reasoning configuration.

## Current State

### acpx CLI already supports `--model`

The acpx CLI has a `--model <id>` global option that applies the model via `session/set_config_option` after session creation. The `set` subcommand also supports `reasoning_effort` and other config options at runtime:

```bash
acpx --model gpt-5.4 codex prompt --session <name> --file -
acpx codex set reasoning_effort xhigh --session <name>
```

### OpenClaw ACPX plugin does not pass `--model`

The ACPX plugin's `buildPromptArgs()` constructs the following command:

```
acpx --format json --json-strict --cwd <dir> \
     --approve-reads --non-interactive-permissions <policy> \
     --ttl <ttl> <agent> prompt --session <name> --file -
```

No `--model` flag is included, and no `setConfigOption` call is made after session creation.

### Plugin config schema has no model/reasoning keys

`AcpxPluginConfigSchema` (strictObject) only supports:

- `command`, `expectedVersion`, `cwd`
- `permissionMode`, `nonInteractivePermissions`
- `pluginToolsMcpBridge`, `strictWindowsCmdWrapper`
- `timeoutSeconds`, `queueOwnerTtlSeconds`
- `mcpServers`

### Agent config overrides only support `command`

`readConfiguredAgentOverrides()` reads only the `command` field from agent entries, ignoring any other properties.

### No programmatic `/acp set` from agents

Agents cannot call `/acp model` or `/acp set` programmatically. These are user-facing slash commands only. `sessions_send` to an ACP session sends a new prompt turn, not a control command.

## Proposal

Add an `agentDefaults` key to the ACPX plugin config schema that allows per-agent default `model` and `configOptions`:

```json5
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "acpx": {
        "config": {
          "agentDefaults": {
            "codex": {
              "model": "gpt-5.4",
              "configOptions": {
                "reasoning_effort": "xhigh"
              }
            },
            "claude": {
              "model": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6",
              "configOptions": {
                "reasoning_effort": "high"
              }
            },
            "gemini": {
              "model": "gemini-3.1-pro"
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Implementation options

**Option A — Pass `--model` at prompt time (minimal change):**

In `buildPromptArgs()`, if `agentDefaults[agent].model` is set, append `--model <id>` to the prefix args. This leverages existing acpx CLI `--model` behavior with a one-line change.

**Option B — Auto `setConfigOption` after session creation (more flexible):**

After `ensureSession()` completes (or on first prompt), automatically call `setConfigOption()` for each key in `agentDefaults[agent].configOptions`. This handles arbitrary config options beyond just `model` (e.g., `reasoning_effort`, `mode`, future agent-specific options).

**Recommendation:** Combine both — Option A for `model` (since acpx already supports `--model`), Option B for `configOptions` (generic key-value pairs applied via `session/set_config_option`).

## Current Workaround

After each ACP session spawn, the operator must manually run:

```
/acp model gpt-5.4
/acp set reasoning_effort xhigh
```

This cannot be automated at the config level and must be repeated for every new session.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.1
- ACPX plugin: bundled with OpenClaw
- Agents tested: codex (codex-acp 0.9.5), claude (claude-agent-acp 0.21.0), gemini CLI

```

This PR adds MCP Resource support to both the plugin-tools server (`src/mcp/plugin-tools-serve.ts` + `src/mcp/plugin-resources.ts`) and the channel server (`src/mcp/channel-server.ts` + `src/mcp/channel-resources.ts`). Resources let MCP clients inspect running OpenClaw state (version, tools list, plugin/MCP config, bridge status, conversations) without issuing tool calls. **Key changes:** - `plugin-resources.ts`: defines four static resources (`openclaw://version`, `openclaw://tools`, `openclaw://config/plugins`, `openclaw://config/mcp`) via a new `McpResourceDefinition` type. Resources are snapshot-frozen at server construction time. - `plugin-tools-serve.ts`: adds `ListResourcesRequestSchema` / `ReadResourceRequestSchema` handlers and declares the `resources` capability. - `channel-resources.ts`: registers three async resources on the `McpServer` (version, bridge status, conversations). - `channel-server.ts`: wires in `registerChannelMcpResources` after the existing tools registration. - `plugin-tools-serve.test.ts`: good coverage of list, read, unknown-URI, and built-in resource presence. **Issues found:** - **Security (P0):** `openclaw://config/mcp` redacts `headers` but not `env`. `McpServerConfig.env` is `Record` and typically holds API keys / auth tokens for stdio-transport MCP servers. These are exposed verbatim to every MCP client that reads this resource. - **Maintainability (P1):** The intentional omission of `plugins.entries[].config` (which can contain plugin secrets) is correct but undocumented. A future maintainer could add it back by mistake.

Confidence Score: 2/5

Not safe to merge as-is — the `openclaw://config/mcp` resource leaks `env` values (API keys/tokens) to MCP clients. The channel-side implementation is clean and the tests are solid, but `plugin-resources.ts` has a concrete secret-leak: `McpServerConfig.env` (commonly holding auth tokens for stdio MCP servers) is serialised verbatim into the `openclaw://config/mcp` resource while only `headers` gets redacted. This needs to be fixed before the feature ships. src/mcp/plugin-resources.ts — specifically the `openclaw://config/mcp` resource redaction logic (lines 93–105).

The current redaction logic only strips `headers`, leaving `env` exposed verbatim to any MCP client that reads `openclaw://config/mcp`. ```suggestion const entry = { ...server } as Record; // Redact sensitive fields if (typeof entry.headers === "object" && entry.headers) { entry.headers = "[redacted]"; } if (typeof entry.env === "object" && entry.env) { entry.env = "[redacted]"; } return [name, entry]; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/mcp/plugin-resources.ts Line: 57-82 Comment: **`plugins.entries[].config` silently omitted without documentation** `PluginEntryConfig.config` is a `Record` that plugins use to store arbitrary configuration, which can include API keys, tokens, or other secrets. The current code intentionally only serialises `enabled` and `hooks` for each entry — this is the right call — but there is no comment explaining why `config` is omitted. Future maintainers may add it back thinking it was simply forgotten, accidentally reintroducing a secret-leak path. Add a brief comment above the mapping to make the omission explicit: ```suggestion Object.entries(plugins.entries).map(([id, entry]) => [ id, { // `entry.config` is intentionally omitted: it may contain plugin secrets/API keys. enabled: entry?.enabled, hooks: entry?.hooks, }, ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. --- This is a comment left during a code review. Path: src/mcp/plugin-resources.ts Line: 12-17 Comment: **`read()` is synchronous — config snapshot is frozen at construction time** `McpResourceDefinition.read` is typed as `() => string`, so resources always return the state captured when `resolvePluginResources` was first called. For long-lived server instances (e.g. if tools are loaded dynamically after startup), `openclaw://tools` and `openclaw://config/*` will silently serve stale data. If live reads are needed in the future, the signature would need to become `() => string | Promise`. This is a minor design note — the current synchronous snapshot is fine for the initial use case — but worth documenting so callers are aware of the constraint: ```suggestion export type McpResourceDefinition = { uri: string; name: string; description: string; mimeType: string; /** * Returns the resource body as a string. Called on every read request. * NOTE: The result reflects the config/tools snapshot captured at server * construction time; live state changes after startup are not reflected. */ read: () => string; }; ``` How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise. `````
